topic: A few questions about the Stage 1 evaluation dataset:

messages.content:
Hi everyone! I'm working on this with a small team. We've built a local testing harness that replicates SAIR's eval setup, which has let us iterate quickly on cheatsheet versions without burning playground credits.

A few questions about the Stage 1 evaluation dataset:

 1. Will the final evaluation problems be drawn from the same 4694-equation universe as the playground problems, or could the equation set be larger/different?
 2. Will the TRUE/FALSE ratio in the final evaluation set be similar to the training set (~50% TRUE for normal, ~37% TRUE for hard)? This matters for calibrating "when uncertain, default to X" guidance in cheatsheets.
3. Will the hard/normal difficulty split be preserved, or will the evaluation be a single undifferentiated set?

Understanding the distribution helps participants decide whether to include explicit bias corrections vs. letting structural rules decide.
messages.topic: A few questions about the Stage 1 evaluation dataset:

messages.content:
We are still discussing 1, but there seems to be a real possibility that the original universe could be completely saturated and so we are considering enlarging to also include some equations of order 5, although the data here is incomplete (not every implication has a known truth value). If so, we will release further test sets.

For 2, we are aiming to generate adversarially hard test sets, for which "easy" methods do not reveal much information about the answer to the problems. In particular, this would mean a 50/50 true false split.

3. We will test with a single test set for the purposes of qualifying for Stage 2, though we may collect additional statistics that may be of interest on the submitted sheets.
messages.topic: A few questions about the Stage 1 evaluation dataset:

messages.content:
Thanks Terence! The 50/50 adversarial split and single test set are exactly what we needed to know. 

A couple follow-ups: 

1. Order-5 truth values: If order-5 equations are included, will evaluation problems be restricted to implications with established truth values (Lean proof or confirmed counterexample), or might some be unresolved? We'd want to avoid building cheats heets that confidently answer undecided cases.
2. "Easy methods": When designing adversarially hard sets, what counts as "easy"? We ask because it would help us understand whether purely structural checks (trivial equation detection, zero-magma alignment, variable balance) are what the adversarial construction is trying to defeat, or whether something more specific is the target. If those structural rules are what's being avoided, we should invest more in deeper reasoning; if it's something else, we'd focus differently.
messages.topic: A few questions about the Stage 1 evaluation dataset:

messages.content:
@_**Terence Tao|28** [said](https://zulip.sair.foundation/#narrow/channel/9-general/topic/A.20few.20questions.20about.20the.20Stage.201.20evaluation.20dataset.3A/near/943):
```quote
original universe could be completely saturated
```
 
Hi Terence,

When you say the original universe could be saturated, do you mean the full 4694-law universe and its induced ~22 million implication pairs, or only the current public/evaluation subsets?

More broadly, is the spirit of the competition to discover and express structural laws of implication that generalize beyond the current universe, or mainly to model the present subset as accurately as possible? I only ask as those seem like quite different directions
messages.topic: A few questions about the Stage 1 evaluation dataset:

messages.content: Initially I thought just being able to accurately describe the initial 22 million implication set was challenging enough, but based on initial results it seems that it may be possible to get very high accuracy on this (though we will soon release some more difficult sets of problems to gauge if this is indeed the case). So we may up the challenge level a bit and include known order 5 implications, but we will not include ones which are unknown as these cannot be scored accurately. From a mathematical point of view it would certainly be of more interest to discover more general accurate heuristics about the implication problem that apply for orders above 4.
messages.topic: A few questions about the Stage 1 evaluation dataset:

messages.content:
@_**Terence Tao|28** [said](https://zulip.sair.foundation/#narrow/channel/9-general/topic/A.20few.20questions.20about.20the.20Stage.201.20evaluation.20dataset.3A/near/984):
```quote
Initially I thought just being able to accurately describe the initial 22 million implication set was challenging enough, but based on initial results it seems that it may be possible to get very high accuracy on this (though we will soon release some more difficult sets of problems to gauge if this is indeed the case). So we may up the challenge level a bit and include known order 5 implications, but we will not include ones which are unknown as these cannot be scored accurately. From a mathematical point of view it would certainly be of more interest to discover more general accurate heuristics about the implication problem that apply for orders above 4.
```
Thanks Terence, that helps. Just to clarify, is there already evidence that these methods are also highly accurate on unseen held-out problems?

````quote
@_**Terence Tao|28** [said](https://zulip.sair.foundation/#narrow/channel/9-general/topic/A.20few.20questions.20about.20the.20Stage.201.20evaluation.20dataset.3A/near/984):
```quote
Initially I thought just being able to accurately describe the initial 22 million implication set was challenging enough, but based on initial results it seems that it may be possible to get very high accuracy on this (though we will soon release some more difficult sets of problems to gauge if this is indeed the case). So we may up the challenge level a bit and include known order 5 implications, but we will not include ones which are unknown as these cannot be scored accurately. From a mathematical point of view it would certainly be of more interest to discover more general accurate heuristics about the implication problem that apply for orders above 4.
```
Thanks Terence, that helps. Just to clarify, is there already evidence that these methods are also highly accurate on unseen held-out problems?
````

To add to this — would it be feasible to publish a simple leaderboard showing top 20 cheatsheet names and their accuracy on a hidden held-out set (say 500-1000 balanced problems), without revealing the actual cheatsheet contents? This would give everyone signal on whether current methods generalize to unseen problems, and help distinguish real performance from playground overfitting. Even just names + scores would be very informative.
